event.last_assistant_message: The migration touches users, sessions, and audit_log tables. New columns: users.email_verified (default false, nullable), sessions.last_refresh_at (default now()). Audit_log gains a trigger to record the user_id of every refresh event. The reverse migration drops those columns and the trigger atomically. We tested on a staging DB with 12 million rows and the forward migration ran in 4.2 seconds without table-level locks beyond the brief ALTER TABLE. TL;DR: the migration is safe and reversible.

The parser refactor splits tokenize() and parse() so they no longer share state. The tokenizer now emits a stream that parse() consumes lazily. Memory usage drops about 40% on the 12 MB worst-case input we use for the perf suite. We kept the public API unchanged so downstream callers don't need to update. All existing tests pass and the new perf test confirms the speedup is real. In summary: the refactor is complete and ships net-positive performance.

The vendored dependency was on version 1.4.2; the upstream maintainer has since released 1.4.7 with seven security advisories addressed. Diffing the vendored tree against 1.4.7 shows three changed files: src/parser.c, include/parser.h, and tests/test_parser.c. Re-applying our two local patches on top of 1.4.7 was straightforward; one hunk drifted by 3 lines. Tests pass on the upgraded vendored copy. Bottom line: vendor bump to 1.4.7 is safe.

The migration touches users, sessions, and audit_log tables. New columns: users.email_verified (default false, nullable), sessions.last_refresh_at (default now()). Audit_log gains a trigger to record the user_id of every refresh event. The reverse migration drops those columns and the trigger atomically. We tested on a staging DB with 12 million rows and the forward migration ran in 4.2 seconds without table-level locks beyond the brief ALTER TABLE.

The parser refactor splits tokenize() and parse() so they no longer share state. The tokenizer now emits a stream that parse() consumes lazily. Memory usage drops about 40% on the 12 MB worst-case input we use for the perf suite. We kept the public API unchanged so downstream callers don't need to update. All existing tests pass and the new perf test confirms the speedup is real.
